name: Iris Flower Classification
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
import pandas as pd
load_code:
data = load_iris()
df = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
df['target'] = data.target
df['species'] = pd.Categorical.from_codes(data.target, data.target_names)
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A classic supervised learning dataset containing measurements of iris flowers. Includes sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width across 3 species: Setosa, Versicolor, and Virginica. Widely used for benchmarking classification algorithms and visualizing decision boundaries.
use_case: Multiclass classification, model benchmarking, decision boundary visualization, beginner ML experiments
features_info: 4 numerical features: sepal_length, sepal_width, petal_length, petal_width in centimeters

name: Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
import pandas as pd
load_code:
data = load_breast_cancer()
df = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
df['target'] = data.target
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A medical dataset for classifying breast tumors as malignant or benign based on 30 computed features from digitized images of fine needle aspirates. Features describe characteristics of cell nuclei including radius, texture, perimeter, area, and smoothness.
use_case: Binary classification, medical diagnosis, feature selection, imbalanced class handling
features_info: 30 numerical features computed from cell nuclei images: mean, standard error, and worst values for radius, texture, perimeter, area, smoothness, compactness, concavity, symmetry, fractal dimension

name: Diabetes Disease Progression
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
import pandas as pd
load_code:
data = load_diabetes()
df = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
df['target'] = data.target
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A regression dataset measuring disease progression in diabetic patients one year after baseline. Features include age, sex, BMI, blood pressure, and six blood serum measurements. Widely used for regression analysis and feature importance studies.
use_case: Regression, medical outcome prediction, feature importance, linear vs nonlinear comparison
features_info: 10 baseline features: age, sex, bmi, bp, s1-s6 (blood serum measurements)

name: Wine Quality Classification
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_wine
import pandas as pd
load_code:
data = load_wine()
df = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
df['target'] = data.target
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A chemical analysis dataset of wines grown in Italy from three cultivars. Contains 13 chemical measurements including alcohol content, malic acid, ash, magnesium, and flavonoids. Used for multiclass classification and chemometric analysis.
use_case: Multiclass classification, chemometrics, feature analysis, dimensionality reduction
features_info: 13 numerical chemical features: alcohol, malic_acid, ash, alcalinity_of_ash, magnesium, total_phenols, flavanoids, nonflavanoid_phenols, proanthocyanins, color_intensity, hue, od280/od315, proline

name: California Housing Prices
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_california_housing
import pandas as pd
load_code:
data = fetch_california_housing()
df = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
df['target'] = data.target
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A real-world dataset derived from the 1990 California census containing housing information from California districts. Features include median income, house age, average rooms, population, and geographic location. The task is to predict median house values.
use_case: Regression, real estate price prediction, geographic feature analysis, ensemble methods
features_info: 8 numerical features: MedInc, HouseAge, AveRooms, AveBedrms, Population, AveOccup, Latitude, Longitude

name: Handwritten Digits Recognition
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
load_code:
data = load_digits()
df = pd.DataFrame(data.data)
df['target'] = data.target
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A dataset of 8x8 pixel grayscale images of handwritten digits 0-9. Each image is flattened into 64 features representing pixel intensities. Used for image classification without deep learning, dimensionality reduction, and clustering experiments.
use_case: Multiclass classification, dimensionality reduction, clustering, image recognition without CNN
features_info: 64 numerical pixel intensity features (0-16), representing 8x8 pixel images

name: Synthetic Binary Classification Dataset
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
import pandas as pd
load_code:
X, y = make_classification(n_samples=1000, n_features=20, n_informative=10, n_redundant=5, random_state=42)
cols = [f'feature_{i}' for i in range(X.shape[1])]
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=cols)
df['target'] = y
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A synthetically generated binary classification dataset with configurable informative features, redundant features, and noise. Designed for testing and benchmarking classifiers under controlled conditions where ground truth is known. Not representative of any specific real-world domain.
use_case: Binary classification algorithm benchmarking, comparing classifiers, testing regularization, evaluating feature selection methods
features_info: 20 anonymous numerical features (10 informative, 5 redundant, 5 noise) — not named for any domain; use real-world datasets for domain-specific problems

name: IBM Telco Customer Churn
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_openml
import pandas as pd
load_code:
telco = fetch_openml(data_id=42178, as_frame=True, parser='auto')
df = telco.frame
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
print(df['Churn'].value_counts())
description: IBM Telco Customer Churn dataset containing 7043 telecom customers with demographic info, account information, and services subscribed. The target is whether the customer churned. Industry-standard benchmark for churn prediction, customer retention modeling, and class-imbalance handling. Features are realistic and domain-specific.
use_case: Customer churn prediction, retention strategy modeling, telecom analytics, class imbalance handling (SMOTE, class_weight), feature importance for churn drivers
features_info: 21 features: tenure, MonthlyCharges, TotalCharges, Contract (month-to-month/one year/two year), PaymentMethod, InternetService, OnlineSecurity, TechSupport, StreamingTV, StreamingMovies, PhoneService, MultipleLines, gender, SeniorCitizen, Partner, Dependents, PaperlessBilling

name: Synthetic Regression Dataset
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import make_regression
import pandas as pd
load_code:
X, y = make_regression(n_samples=1000, n_features=20, n_informative=10, noise=0.1, random_state=42)
cols = [f'feature_{i}' for i in range(X.shape[1])]
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=cols)
df['target'] = y
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A synthetically generated regression dataset with configurable informative features and noise. Represents scenarios like sales forecasting, demand prediction, or price estimation with controllable complexity.
use_case: Regression, sales forecasting, demand prediction, feature importance, regularization experiments
features_info: 20 numerical features (10 informative, 10 noise), continuous target

name: Synthetic Clustering Dataset
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs
import pandas as pd
load_code:
X, y = make_blobs(n_samples=500, n_features=2, centers=4, cluster_std=1.0, random_state=42)
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=['feature_1', 'feature_2'])
df['cluster'] = y
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A synthetically generated clustering dataset with configurable cluster centers, standard deviation, and number of features. Ideal for visualizing and testing clustering algorithms like K-Means, DBSCAN, and Gaussian Mixture Models.
use_case: Clustering, unsupervised learning, customer segmentation, anomaly detection
features_info: 2-N numerical features forming distinct Gaussian clusters

name: Synthetic Non-Linear Classification (Moons)
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import make_moons
import pandas as pd
load_code:
X, y = make_moons(n_samples=500, noise=0.1, random_state=42)
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=['feature_1', 'feature_2'])
df['target'] = y
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A synthetic two-dimensional dataset shaped like two interleaving half-circles. Specifically designed to test non-linear classifiers. Linear models fail on this dataset while kernel SVM, neural networks, and ensemble methods succeed.
use_case: Non-linear classification, kernel methods, neural networks, decision boundary visualization
features_info: 2 numerical features forming two interleaved half-moon shapes

name: Synthetic Concentric Circles Classification
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import make_circles
import pandas as pd
load_code:
X, y = make_circles(n_samples=500, noise=0.05, factor=0.5, random_state=42)
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=['feature_1', 'feature_2'])
df['target'] = y
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())
description: A synthetic dataset with two concentric circles representing a non-linearly separable binary classification problem. Tests the ability of models to learn circular decision boundaries. Ideal for demonstrating kernel tricks and radial basis functions.
use_case: Non-linear classification, kernel SVM, radial basis functions, decision boundary comparison
features_info: 2 numerical features arranged in concentric circular patterns

name: 20 Newsgroups Text Classification
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_20newsgroups
import pandas as pd
load_code:
data = fetch_20newsgroups(subset='all', remove=('headers', 'footers', 'quotes'))
df = pd.DataFrame({'text': data.data, 'target': data.target})
df['category'] = [data.target_names[t] for t in data.target]
print(df.shape)
print(df['category'].value_counts())
description: A collection of approximately 20,000 newsgroup documents across 20 different topics including technology, sports, politics, religion, and science. A benchmark dataset for text classification, topic modeling, and information retrieval.
use_case: Text classification, topic modeling, TF-IDF, NLP pipelines, multi-label classification
features_info: Raw text documents from 20 newsgroup categories

name: Olivetti Faces Recognition

use_case: Face recognition, dimensionality reduction, PCA, clustering, semi-supervised learning
features_info: 4096 numerical pixel intensity features representing 64x64 grayscale face images

name: Sunspot Activity Time Series

data.columns = ['year', 'sunspots']
data['year'] = data['year'].astype(int)

description: Historical annual sunspot activity counts from 1700 to 2008. Exhibits well-known 11-year solar cycles. Used for cyclic time-series forecasting, spectral analysis, and autocorrelation studies.
use_case: Time-series forecasting, cyclic pattern detection, ARIMA, spectral analysis, autocorrelation
features_info: 2 columns: year (1700-2008), sunspots (annual count) — clear 11-year solar cycle

name: MNIST Handwritten Digits
import_code:
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import mnist
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
load_code:
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = mnist.load_data()
x_train = x_train.astype('float32') / 255.0
x_test = x_test.astype('float32') / 255.0
print(f'Train: {x_train.shape}, Test: {x_test.shape}')
plt.imshow(x_train[0], cmap='gray')
plt.show()
description: The benchmark image classification dataset containing 70,000 grayscale 28x28 images of handwritten digits 0-9. Split into 60,000 training and 10,000 test images. The standard starting point for deep learning and CNN experiments.
use_case: Image classification, CNN training, deep learning fundamentals, neural network experiments
features_info: 28x28 grayscale pixel images, 10 digit classes (0-9)

name: Fashion MNIST Clothing Classification
import_code:
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import fashion_mnist
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
load_code:
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = fashion_mnist.load_data()
x_train = x_train.astype('float32') / 255.0
x_test = x_test.astype('float32') / 255.0
class_names = ['T-shirt', 'Trouser', 'Pullover', 'Dress', 'Coat', 'Sandal', 'Shirt', 'Sneaker', 'Bag', 'Ankle boot']
print(f'Train: {x_train.shape}, Test: {x_test.shape}')
description: A harder drop-in replacement for MNIST containing 70,000 grayscale images of 10 clothing categories including T-shirts, trousers, coats, sneakers, and bags. More challenging than digit MNIST while maintaining the same format.
use_case: Image classification, CNN training, fashion retail AI, transfer learning, harder MNIST alternative
features_info: 28x28 grayscale clothing images, 10 categories: T-shirt, Trouser, Pullover, Dress, Coat, Sandal, Shirt, Sneaker, Bag, Ankle boot

name: CIFAR-10 Object Recognition
import_code:
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import cifar10
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
load_code:
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = cifar10.load_data()
x_train = x_train.astype('float32') / 255.0
x_test = x_test.astype('float32') / 255.0
class_names = ['airplane','automobile','bird','cat','deer','dog','frog','horse','ship','truck']
print(f'Train: {x_train.shape}, Test: {x_test.shape}')
description: 60,000 32x32 color images in 10 object categories: airplanes, automobiles, birds, cats, deer, dogs, frogs, horses, ships, and trucks. A standard benchmark for evaluating deep CNN architectures and data augmentation techniques.
use_case: Image classification, CNN architecture design, data augmentation, transfer learning, object recognition
features_info: 32x32 RGB color images, 10 object categories

name: CIFAR-100 Fine-Grained Recognition
import_code:
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import cifar100
import numpy as np
load_code:
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = cifar100.load_data()
x_train = x_train.astype('float32') / 255.0
x_test = x_test.astype('float32') / 255.0
print(f'Train: {x_train.shape}, Test: {x_test.shape}')
description: Similar to CIFAR-10 but with 100 fine-grained classes grouped into 20 superclasses. Includes animals, vehicles, household items, food, and more. Tests fine-grained visual recognition and hierarchical classification.
use_case: Fine-grained classification, hierarchical classification, transfer learning, advanced CNN
features_info: 32x32 RGB color images, 100 fine-grained classes in 20 superclasses

name: IMDB Movie Sentiment Reviews
import_code:
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import imdb
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.sequence import pad_sequences
import numpy as np
load_code:
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = imdb.load_data(num_words=10000)
x_train = pad_sequences(x_train, maxlen=200)
x_test = pad_sequences(x_test, maxlen=200)
print(f'Train: {x_train.shape}, Test: {x_test.shape}')
description: 25,000 highly polar movie reviews from IMDB labeled as positive or negative. Reviews are pre-tokenized as integer sequences representing word indices. Standard benchmark for sentiment analysis and RNN/LSTM experiments.
use_case: Sentiment analysis, LSTM training, text classification, RNN, embedding layers
features_info: Integer sequences representing tokenized movie reviews, top 10000 vocabulary words

name: Reuters News Topic Classification
import_code:
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import reuters
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.sequence import pad_sequences
import numpy as np
load_code:
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = reuters.load_data(num_words=10000)
x_train = pad_sequences(x_train, maxlen=200)
x_test = pad_sequences(x_test, maxlen=200)
print(f'Train: {x_train.shape}, Test: {x_test.shape}')
print(f'Classes: {len(set(y_train))}')
description: A dataset of 11,228 Reuters newswire articles categorized into 46 topics including earnings, acquisitions, interest rates, and trade. Pre-tokenized into integer sequences. Used for multi-class text classification.
use_case: Multiclass text classification, news categorization, LSTM, topic detection
features_info: Integer sequences of newswire articles, 46 topic categories

name: NLTK Movie Reviews Sentiment Corpus
import_code:
import nltk
nltk.download('movie_reviews')
from nltk.corpus import movie_reviews
import pandas as pd
load_code:
docs = [(list(movie_reviews.words(fileid)), category)
        for category in movie_reviews.categories()
        for fileid in movie_reviews.fileids(category)]
docs_df = pd.DataFrame(docs, columns=['words', 'sentiment'])
docs_df['text'] = docs_df['words'].apply(lambda x: ' '.join(x))
print(docs_df.shape)
print(docs_df['sentiment'].value_counts())
description: 2,000 movie review documents from the NLTK corpus, balanced between 1,000 positive and 1,000 negative reviews. Tokenized and categorized. A classic NLP benchmark for bag-of-words, TF-IDF, and naive Bayes classifiers.
use_case: Sentiment analysis, bag-of-words, TF-IDF, naive Bayes, classic NLP pipelines
features_info: Raw tokenized text from 2000 movie reviews, balanced positive and negative classes

name: Brown Corpus - POS Tagging
import_code:
import nltk
nltk.download('brown')
from nltk.corpus import brown
import pandas as pd
load_code:
tagged_sents = brown.tagged_sents(tagset='universal')
data = [(word, tag) for sent in tagged_sents[:5000] for word, tag in sent]
df = pd.DataFrame(data, columns=['word', 'pos_tag'])
print(df.shape)
print(df['pos_tag'].value_counts())
description: One of the oldest English language corpora containing over 1 million words from 500 American English texts. Each word is tagged with part-of-speech labels. Used for POS tagging, language modeling, and NLP fundamentals.
use_case: POS tagging, language modeling, sequence labeling, NLP fundamentals, text statistics
features_info: Word tokens with universal POS tags: NOUN, VERB, ADJ, ADV, DET, ADP, CONJ, PRON, PRT, NUM, X

name: IMDB Movie Reviews (HuggingFace)
import_code:
from datasets import load_dataset
import pandas as pd
load_code:
dataset = load_dataset('imdb')
df_train = pd.DataFrame(dataset['train'])
df_test = pd.DataFrame(dataset['test'])
print(df_train.shape)
print(df_train['label'].value_counts())
description: 50,000 highly polar movie reviews from IMDB in raw text format via HuggingFace datasets. Balanced between 25,000 positive and 25,000 negative reviews. Ready for transformer fine-tuning with BERT and GPT models.
use_case: Sentiment analysis, BERT fine-tuning, transformer training, text classification, LLM experiments
features_info: Raw text movie reviews with binary sentiment labels (0=negative, 1=positive)

name: AG News Topic Classification
import_code:
from datasets import load_dataset
import pandas as pd
load_code:
dataset = load_dataset('ag_news')
df = pd.DataFrame(dataset['train'])
df['category'] = df['label'].map({0:'World', 1:'Sports', 2:'Business', 3:'Sci/Tech'})
print(df.shape)
print(df['category'].value_counts())
description: A large collection of 120,000 news articles from more than 2,000 sources categorized into 4 classes: World, Sports, Business, and Science/Technology. Used for text classification benchmarking with balanced class distribution.
use_case: Multiclass text classification, news categorization, transformer fine-tuning, TF-IDF baseline
features_info: News article titles and descriptions, 4 balanced topic categories

name: Twitter Emotion Detection
import_code:
from datasets import load_dataset
import pandas as pd
load_code:
dataset = load_dataset('dair-ai/emotion')
df = pd.DataFrame(dataset['train'])
emotion_names = ['sadness', 'joy', 'love', 'anger', 'fear', 'surprise']
df['emotion'] = df['label'].map({i: e for i, e in enumerate(emotion_names)})
print(df.shape)
print(df['emotion'].value_counts())

name: Zachary Karate Club Graph Network

print(f'Node features: {data.x.shape}')
print(f'Labels: {data.y}')
description: Classic graph benchmark from Zachary's 1977 social network study of a university karate club. 34 nodes (members), 78 edges (friendships), 4 community labels. The 'hello world' of graph neural networks (GNNs).
use_case: Graph neural networks, node classification, community detection, GCN/GAT tutorial
features_info: 34 nodes, 78 edges, 34-dimensional one-hot node features, 4 class labels

print(f'Features: {data.x.shape}, Classes: {dataset.num_classes}')
description: Scientific citation network where nodes are research papers (2708) and edges are citation links (5429). Each paper has a 1433-dimensional bag-of-words feature vector and belongs to one of 7 research topics. Standard GNN benchmark used in GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE papers.
use_case: Node classification, graph convolutional networks, semi-supervised learning on graphs, link prediction
features_info: 2708 nodes (papers), 5429 edges (citations), 1433 BoW features per node, 7 class labels

print(X.shape)
description: Full Titanic passenger manifest with survival labels, including additional fields not in the Seaborn version (cabin, ticket number, etc.). Classic binary classification benchmark with rich feature engineering opportunities.
use_case: Binary classification, missing value imputation, feature engineering, gradient boosting, logistic regression
features_info: Pclass, Sex, Age, SibSp, Parch, Fare, Embarked, Cabin, Ticket; 1309 samples

name: WinoGrande Commonsense Reasoning Benchmark
import_code: from datasets import load_dataset
load_code:
dataset = load_dataset('winogrande', 'winogrande_xl', split='train[:500]', trust_remote_code=True)
print(dataset)
print(dataset[0])
description: Large-scale Winograd schema challenge dataset requiring commonsense reasoning to resolve pronoun ambiguity. 44,000 problems at various difficulty levels. Designed to be adversarially hard for models exploiting statistical biases.
use_case: Commonsense reasoning evaluation, LLM benchmarking, pronoun coreference resolution, NLU fine-tuning
features_info: Sentence with blank, two candidate options (option1, option2), answer label

features_info: Title, text, domain, date, description; 708k+ articles

name: Poker Hand Classification (OpenML)
import_code:
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_openml
import pandas as pd
load_code:
poker = fetch_openml('poker-hand', version=1, as_frame=True, parser='auto')
X, y = poker.data, poker.target
print(X.head())
print(y.value_counts())
description: Each instance is 5 playing cards represented by suit and rank integers. The target is the poker hand category (nothing, pair, two pairs, three of a kind, etc.). A classic multi-class benchmark with extreme class imbalance (royal flush is very rare).
use_case: Multi-class classification, class imbalance handling, ensemble methods, feature engineering from card combinations
features_info: 10 features (suit S1-S5, rank C1-C5), 10 poker hand classes, 1M training + 25k test instances

name: PubMed Biomedical Article Summarization
import_code: from datasets import load_dataset
load_code:
dataset = load_dataset('ccdv/pubmed-summarization', split='train[:300]', trust_remote_code=True)
print(dataset)
print(dataset[0].keys())
description: 133,215 biomedical research papers from PubMed with their structured abstracts as reference summaries. The articles field contains full paper text; the abstract field is the gold summary. Standard benchmark for long-document summarisation.
use_case: Abstractive summarisation, long-document NLP, biomedical text processing, LLM fine-tuning for scientific content
features_info: Full article text + abstract pairs from PubMed; biomedical domain; avg article length ~3000 tokens

name: Stanford Cars Fine-Grained Vehicle Classification

load_code:
dataset = load_dataset('tanganke/stanford_cars', split='train[:200]')
print(dataset)
print(dataset[0].keys())
description: 16,185 images of 196 car classes (make, model, year) such as '2012 Tesla Model S' and '2012 BMW M3 Coupe'. Images contain bounding box annotations. A popular fine-grained visual recognition benchmark where classes require expert-level distinction.
use_case: Fine-grained visual recognition, transfer learning, vehicle classification, bounding box regression
features_info: 196 car classes (make/model/year), bounding box annotations, 8144 train / 8041 test images

name: Stanford Natural Language Inference (SNLI)
import_code: from datasets import load_dataset
load_code:
dataset = load_dataset('snli', split='train[:1000]')
print(dataset)
print(dataset[0])
description: 570,000 human-written English sentence pairs manually labelled for entailment, contradiction, or neutral. The foundational benchmark for natural language inference (NLI). Used to pretrain and evaluate BERT, RoBERTa, and other transformers.
use_case: Natural language inference, sentence embedding evaluation, textual entailment, transformer fine-tuning
features_info: Premise and hypothesis sentence pairs, 3 labels: entailment / contradiction / neutral
